title: IBM Opens, Customizes Power Chips
author: Jay Lyman, TechNewsWorld
abstract: TechNewsWorld article (Apr 1 2004, Jay Lyman) on IBM's Power Everywhere event in New York announcing opening of the Power microprocessor architecture to external customization, with China's Culturecom cited as first-mover (Chinese-language-character customization). IBM spokesperson Jim Larkin: 'We want to see the customers themselves do the customization on the microprocessor architecture, and that's never happened before.' Aberdeen chief research analyst Peter Kastner provides the strategic read: 'No one today considers designing a new product by designing the processor to run it, so IBM wants to sell more Power processor cores while not shoving a full-blown microprocessor design down designers' throats.' Kastner frames the play as IBM fighting Intel's two powerhouse architectures (x86 and XScale) for silicon in the next generation of consumer devices — cell phones, set-top boxes, cameras: 'Customization could be done economically and with low technology risk.' Gartner's Martin Reynolds adds the chip-redesign-cost context: 'It's going to be incredibly expensive to redesign chips.'
importance_rationale: Documents the launch of Power.org / Power Everywhere — IBM's 2004 attempt to turn Power into an open, ARM-style embedded-licensed architecture. The event represented IBM's strategic response to the ARM mobile-chip tsunami. Kastner's analyst framing captured the business rationale exactly.
relevance_rationale: IBM's embedded-Power bid largely failed (ARM dominates mobile/embedded; Power remains niche in AIX/IBM i servers), but the open-chip-architecture pattern Kastner identified returned as RISC-V from 2015 onward — now the fastest-growing open ISA. Apple Silicon (2020), AWS Graviton, and NVIDIA Grace are modern chip-customization successes in the vein IBM attempted.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's core insight — that chip-customization-as-a-service is strategically important and IBM was well-positioned technologically — was durable. But IBM's specific embedded-Power bid largely failed against ARM in mobile/embedded. Power.org disbanded circa 2013; Power lingered in servers (IBM i, AIX) and briefly in the PlayStation 3 Cell and Xbox 360 Xenon, then retreated. The customization thesis was ultimately realized by RISC-V and ARM, not Power.
